title: Vector Databases & Search
learning_outcomes: Explain HNSW skip-list graph traversal mechanics.
Formulate Inverted File Product Quantization (IVF-PQ) memory compression steps.

content_sections.title: Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) Graphs
content_sections.content:
To query high-dimensional embeddings efficiently, vector databases implement Approximate Nearest Neighbors (ANN) indices. 

**HNSW** is a multi-layer graph based on skip-lists. Top layers have sparse connections for fast coarse routing across large distances, while bottom layers have dense connections for fine-grained search. Traversing down the hierarchy achieves $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ search complexity.

content_sections.title: Inverted File Product Quantization (IVF-PQ) Compression
content_sections.content:
Storing raw float embeddings requires significant memory. **IVF-PQ** optimizes this storage footprint:

1.  **Inverted File (IVF):** Groups vectors into Voronoi cells using K-Means. During query retrieval, the search is restricted to the closest centroids, reducing the search space.
2.  **Product Quantization (PQ):** Compresses high-dimensional vectors. It splits a $d$-dimensional vector into $m$ sub-vectors of dimension $d/m$. It clusters each sub-space independently using K-Means to build a codebook. Each sub-vector is then replaced by its nearest centroid index. This compresses a 1024-byte float vector to a small sequence of 8-bit cluster indices, reducing memory usage by 90% or more.
